title: On Clipse's 'Let God Sort Em Out,' Coke Raps, Confessions & Incendiary Bars: 5 Takeaways From The Duo's Reunion Album
text: Grammy.com provides five key takeaways from Clipse's reunion album Let God Sort Em Out. Masters of Drug-Themed Rap: The Virginia brothers reestablish their dominance in cocaine-focused lyricism. Despite their limited actual involvement in the trade, they've built careers on creative references to the subject. Malice Reclaims His Edge: After years in Pusha T's shadow with a softer focus on faith and family, Malice has recovered his gangster rap prowess. His comeback verses prove he remains a formidable lyricist, delivering sharp wordplay throughout the album. Pharrell's Production Balances Past and Present: The producer crafts skeletal beats evoking their 2000s classics while incorporating more dramatic choral and orchestral elements for sophistication. Recording sessions split between Virginia Beach and Paris reflect their evolution. Deeply Personal Grief: The opening track confronts the 2021 deaths of both brothers' parents. Pusha regrets missing his mother's final message; Malice describes discovering his father's body. This vulnerability distinguishes the album from their typical bravado. Uncompromising Aggression: Clipse weaponize their platform against contemporary rap, criticizing gossipy discourse and dissing major artists including Travis Scott.
